nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated species inhabiting reef flats and outer reef slopes of continental reefs at depths of roughly 1–50 m. Has been documented occupying holes and crevices, often shared with cleaner organisms.

decorPreferences: very large, stable rockwork with caves and crevices large enough to conceal the entire body
secure rockwork anchored against collapse (an active eel can dislodge unsecured rock)
tightly sealed, weighted or mechanically secured lid with no gaps
long footprint over depth — at least ~240 cm length is generally advised for adult housing

diet.notes: A piscivorous and teuthivorous predator that has been documented feeding on small fishes, cephalopods (octopus, squid), and larger crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts meaty marine foods such as whole fish, silversides, squid, octopus, krill, and shrimp; newly imported individuals may initially require live feeder fish to trigger a feeding response. Feeding with tongs or a long feeding stick is strongly advised — the species has poor eyesight, relies heavily on smell, and has been known to bite hands by accident. Large meals every 3–4 days tend to be sufficient for adults; juveniles can be fed somewhat more frequently.

goodWith: very large, robust predatory tankmates not small enough to be swallowed
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole (will be eaten)
all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, lobsters, hermit crabs (natural prey)
other moray eels in all but the largest exhibits
conspecifics (territorial; aggression toward conspecifics has been widely reported)

summary: The Tessalata Eel (Gymnothorax favagineus), also known as the Honeycomb Moray, is one of the two largest Indo-Pacific moray species, reaching roughly 2–3 m. Its striking white-with-black honeycomb pattern is unmistakable, but its adult size, aggressive disposition, and predatory diet make it suitable only for very large public-style aquaria.
careNotes:
The Tessalata Eel is widely considered an expert-level species despite many retailers rating its day-to-day care as 'easy.' The discrepancy reflects a common pattern: the animal itself is hardy and forgiving on water quality, but its ultimate adult size (commonly 2 m, reported up to 3 m), aggressive predatory temperament, and very strong escape tendencies place it well outside the scope of typical home aquaria.

A tightly sealed, weighted or mechanically secured lid is generally considered essential — the species is a documented escape artist capable of lifting unweighted covers. Robust biological and mechanical filtration plus a strong protein skimmer are commonly advised given the eel's heavy bioload and large feedings.

It is not generally considered reef-safe in the conventional sense. Some retail sources rate it 'reef safe with caution,' but in practice the species will consume any crustacean and any fish small enough to swallow, and its bulk can dislodge rockwork and corals. Pairing with corals is occasionally attempted in very large public-style exhibits but is not advised in typical reef systems.

Tank-size cross-source disagreement was significant. LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain list a 180-gallon (~680 L) minimum; Reeflings recommends 750 L; ReefApp recommends 2000 L; multiple care guides recommend 300+ gallons (~1140 L) with 500+ gallons (~1900 L) for adults. Given the species' confirmed 2–3 m adult length, ~20 cm/year growth rate, active swimming behavior, and territorial nature, the 2000 L (~528 gal) figure was chosen as the cautious minimum — the smaller retailer figures clearly describe juvenile housing rather than the adult animal.

Adult-size cross-source agreement was strong: FishBase reports 300 cm maximum total length; ReefApp documents up to 300 cm; Reeflings reports ~200 cm typical; AquariumDomain reports up to 178 cm (70 in) and ~6 ft typical. The recorded range of 180–300 cm spans the consensus.

Temperature sources were consistent within a moderate spread: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22–26°C), AquariumDomain 72–80°F (22–27°C), Reeflings 22–28°C, FishBase wild data 24.5–29.1°C, ReefApp ~26°C. Averaged usable range 23–27°C.

Care-level disagreement was substantial: LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain rate the species 'easy'; this profile rates it 'expert' because the size, escape risk, and predatory diet justify the upgrade despite the animal's biological hardiness. No taxonomic ambiguity. No lifespan data was located across the sources consulted.

Ciguatera poisoning has been documented in human consumption of this species in the wild, but this is not relevant to aquarium husbandry.
breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding in the sources reviewed. Reproduction in the wild is by external fertilization; aquarium reproduction is generally considered not achieved under home or even public-aquarium conditions for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page: care level easy, temperament semi-aggressive, reef compatible 'with caution', 180 gal minimum, 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. Listed max size 5–10 in reflects sale-size juveniles, not adult length.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution, adult size, and depth/temperature range. Family Muraenidae, order Anguilliformes; max length 300 cm TL; depth 1–50 m; temperature 24.5–29.1°C; Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/East Africa to PNG, north to southern Japan, south to Australia; feeds on cephalopods and small fishes; IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked: care level easy (disagreement noted), temperament aggressive, 180 gal minimum with 300+ gal recommended, 72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, adult size up to ~178 cm. Not reef-safe. Tight-lid escape risk confirmed.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 2000 L minimum (largest recommendation), temp ~26°C, SG ~1.026, adult size up to 300 cm at ~20 cm/year, reef safe with caution, aggressive, fed every 3–4 days. Indo-Pacific range confirmed.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 750 L minimum, 22–28°C, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.020–1.026, adult size ~200 cm, aggressive/territorial, best kept alone, not reef-safe, prefers live food but adapts to frozen.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented in reviewed sources.
